explicit_text: Turner's Chapel AME was one of the churches where we held mass meetings during the civil rights movement. Reverend Tucker, who was the pastor there, was a young and well-liked Methodist minister. The church was a gathering place for many of us, and it played a role in organizing marches downtown where Reverend Tucker even got bitten by a dog during one protest.

explicit_text: Greenwood, Mississippi was where much of the civil rights activity I was involved in took place. We held mass meetings at local churches like Turner's Chapel AME and Wesley's Chapel ME, and marched downtown despite the dangers. It was a tough time with arrests, dogs, and threats, but the community came together to fight for justice.

explicit_text: I worked at the Presbyterian kindergarten in Greenwood, which was mostly for white children. After I joined the marches, they let me go because they didn't want people involved in the protests working there. It was one of the many ways people faced consequences for standing up in the movement.

explicit_text: My husband Reverend Allen had a barbershop on Johnson Street in Greenwood. He rented the place and was a Baptist preacher, though at that time he was mainly cutting hair. After some trouble with a brick thrown through the shop, the landlord made him leave, showing how the movement affected our lives.

explicit_text: Black Annie was the old black bus used by authorities to transport arrested protesters to the county farm or jail. It was a symbol of the repression we faced during the civil rights movement. Many of us feared being put on Black Annie and taken away just for standing up for our rights.

entity: I Won't Let Nobody Turn Me Around
explicit_text: The song 'I Won't Let Nobody Turn Me Around' was a freedom song we sang to keep our spirits up while in jail. Bob Moses told me that when he was in jail and started singing it, others would draw knives on him, showing how tense and dangerous those times were. The song was a powerful symbol of our determination.

explicit_text: Philadelphia, Mississippi is where they found the bodies of the three boys—James Chaney, Mickey Schwerner, and Andrew Goodman—who were murdered during the civil rights struggle. Bill Hodges and Robert Masters were watching TV when the news came, and I was cooking dinner. It was a heartbreaking moment that showed the deadly risks we faced.
